file_name: Principal Life Insurance Company - Broker Dealer Marketing and Servicing Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: Broker Dealer agrees to maintain all books and records relating to the servicing and sale of Annuity Contracts or interests therein required under the 1934 Act, and any applicable rules promulgated thereunder, and applicable securities or insurance laws of any states.
perturbation.changed_text: Broker Dealer agrees to maintain all books and records relating to the servicing and sale of Annuity Contracts or interests therein for a period of two years, as required under the 1934 Act, and any applicable rules promulgated thereunder, and applicable securities or insurance laws of any states.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the record-keeping duration to 'two years' creates a conflict with SEC rules that require longer retention periods for certain broker-dealer records.
perturbation.contradicted_law: SEC Rule 17a-4(b) under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 17a-4(b) mandates that broker-dealers preserve certain records for at least three years, and in some cases longer (e.g., lifetime of the enterprise for organizational documents). A two-year retention period violates this requirement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: eCFR :: 17 CFR 240.17a-4 -- Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record-keeping duration to two years, which directly contradicts the SEC rule 17a-4(b) mentioned in the Law Explanation, which requires certain records to be preserved for at least three years. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 17 CFR 240.17a-4, indicating its relevance. The Perturbation Explanation also states that this change creates a conflict with SEC rules requiring longer retention periods, reinforcing the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Issuer and Distributor may change the compensation schedules set forth in the attached Exhibits at any time and will notify Broker Dealer of the revised compensation schedules electronically or by other writing in advance of the effective date of the change.
perturbation.changed_text: Issuer and Distributor may change the compensation schedules set forth in the attached Exhibits at any time and will notify Broker Dealer of the revised compensation schedules electronically or by other writing one day in advance of the effective date of the change.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the advance notice for compensation changes to 'one day' can be problematic under FINRA rules, which require fair dealing and can be interpreted to require reasonable notice for significant compensation changes.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly mandating a specific notice period, FINRA Rule 2310 requires fair and reasonable compensation arrangements. A one-day notice for compensation changes could be seen as unfair and unreasonable, potentially violating this rule.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: BrokerCheck - Find a broker, investment or financial advisor
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the advance notice for compensation changes to 'one day,' which the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest is likely to violate FINRA Rule 2310, requiring fair and reasonable compensation arrangements. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful here, the explanations clearly state that one day's notice could be seen as unfair and unreasonable. This makes the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition applicable because the change introduces a time-sensitive obligation (advance notice) that arguably violates federal regulations.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Iowa.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California introduces potential inconsistencies if the contract's terms violate California's specific regulations on insurance or securities, which may differ significantly from Iowa law. This may relate to suitability requirements or disclosure requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Insurance Code, specifically related to annuity suitability
perturbation.law_explanation: California Insurance Code § 10509.6 et seq. has specific requirements regarding annuity suitability, particularly for seniors. If the contract lacks provisions compliant with these requirements, designating California law as governing creates a conflict where the contract terms are unenforceable, and Broker-Dealer actions are exposed to additional regulatory scrutiny.

CLASSES OF INSURANCE [1880 - 12880.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) PART 2. LIFE AND DISABILITY INSURANCE [10110 - 11549] ( Part 2 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) CHAPTER 5. General Regulation of Life Insurers [10430 - 10509.946] ( Chapter 5 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) ARTICLE 8. Requirements for Replacement of Life Insurance and Annuity Policies [10509 - 10509.9] ( Article 8 added by Stats. 1990, Ch. 963, Sec. 1. ) 10509.6. Every life insurer that uses an agent in a life insurance or annuity sale shall do the following: (a) Require with or as part of each completed application for life insurance or annuity, a statement signed by the agent as to whether he or she knows a replacement is or may be involved in the transaction. (b) Where a replacement is involved: (1) Require from the agent with the application for life insurance or annuity: (i) a list of all of the applicant’s existing life insurance or annuity to be replaced, and (ii) a copy of the replacement notice provided the applicant pursuant to Section 10509.4. The existing life insurance or annuity shall be identified by name of insurer, insured, and contract number. If a number has not been assigned by the existing insurer, alternative identificatio
perturbation.justification: The changed text designates California law as governing, while the original designated Iowa law. California Insurance Code § 10509.6, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, outlines specific requirements for life insurers regarding annuity sales, particularly concerning replacements and agent responsibilities. If the contract, under California law, fails to meet these requirements, a legal contradiction arises where the contract's terms become unenforceable, and Broker-Dealer actions are exposed to additional regulatory scrutiny, as stated in the Law Explanation. The original contract under Iowa law would not be subject to these specific California regulations, hence the change creates a contradiction if the contract doesn't comply with California's requirements.
